Fast Planner

Fast PlannerAbstractKinodynamic Path SearchingKinodynamic Path Searching AlgorithmPrimitives GenerationActual Cost and Heuristic CostAnalytic ExpansionOptimality and CompletenessPMP DerivationB-spline Trajectory OptimizationUniform B-splineConvex Hull PropertyProblem FormulationTime AdjustmentNon-uniform B-splineVelocity Knot Span AdjustmentAcceleration Knot Span AdjustmentIterative Time Adjustment Algorithm

Abstract

In this paper, a robust and efficient quadrotor motion planning system is proposed for fast flight in 3-D complex environments.

The paper first adopts a kinodynamic path searching method to find a safe, kinodynamic feasible, and minimum-time initial trajectory in the discretized control space.

Then, the smoothness and clearance of the trajectory are improved by a B-spline optimization, which incorporates gradient information from a Euclidean distance field (EDF) and dynamic constraints efficiently utilizing the convex hull property of B-spline.

Finally, by representing the final trajectory as a non-uniform B-spline, the paper adopts an iterative time adjustment method to guarantee dynamically feasible and non-conservative trajectories.

Kinodynamic Path Searching

Kinodynamic Path Searching Algorithm

The searching loop is similar to the standard A* algorithm, where P and C refer to the open and closed set. Instead of straight lines, motion primitives respecting the quadrotor dynamic are used as graph edge. A structure Node is used to record a primitive, the voxel the primitives ends in and the gc and fc cost.

​x
Initialize open set P and closed set C;


while P is not empty do
    n_c <- P.pop();
    C.insert(n_c);


    if ReachGoal(n_c) or AnalyticExpand(n_c) then
        return RetrievePath();


    primitives <- Expand(n_c);
    nodes <- Prune(primitives);


    for each n_i in nodes do
        if n_i not in C and CheckFeasible(n_i) then
            g_temp <- n_c.g_c + EdgeCost(n_i);


            if n_i not in P then
                P.add(n_i);
            else if g_temp >= n_i.g_c then
                continue;


            n_i.parent <- n_c;
            n_i.g_c <- g_temp;
            n_i.f_c <- n_i.g_c + Heuristic(n_i);

Expand() applies discretized control inputs for duration τ and generates dynamically consistent motion primitives. Primitives Expand() the voxel grid map iteratively.

Prune() removes primitives ending in the same voxel except the one with the smallest fc.

CheckFeasible() checks safety and dynamic feasibility of the remained primitives.

AnalyticExpand() directly connects the current state xc to the goal state xg using the closed-form trajectory above; if the connection is safe and dynamically feasible, the search terminates early.

Primitives Generation

We first discuss the generation of motion primitives used in Expand().

The differential flatness of quadrotor systems allows the trajectory to be represented by three independent 1-D time-parameterized polynomial functions

p(t):=[px(t)py(t)pz(t)]T，pμ(t)=∑k=0Kaktk

where

μ∈{x,y,z}

From the view of quadrotor systems, this corresponds to a linear time-invariant (LTI) system. Define the state vector and control input as

x(t):=[p(t)Tp˙(t)T⋯p(n−1)(t)T]T∈X⊂R3n
u(t):=p(n)(t)∈U:=[−umax,umax]3⊂R3

The state-space model is

x˙=Ax+Bu
A=[0I30⋯000I3⋯0⋮⋮⋮⋱⋮0⋯⋯0I30⋯⋯00],B=[00⋮0I3]

For the linear time-invariant system

x˙(t)=Ax(t)+Bu(t),

where A and B are constant matrices, the state transition can be derived by using the matrix exponential.

First, multiply both sides by e−At

e−Atx˙(t)=e−AtAx(t)+e−AtBu(t)

Since

ddt(e−Atx(t))=e−Atx˙(t)−e−AtAx(t)

we obtain

ddt(e−Atx(t))=e−AtBu(t)

Integrating both sides from 0 to t gives

e−Atx(t)−x(0)=∫0te−AτBu(τ)dτ

Therefore, the complete solution of the state equation is

x(t)=eAtx(0)+∫0teA(t−τ)Bu(τ)dτ

In Expand(), given the current quadrotor state, a set of discretized control inputs UD⊂U is applied for duration τ. In practice n=2, which corresponds to a double integrator.

x(t)=[p(t)v(t)]u(t)=a(t)

Each axis is uniformly discretized as

[−umax,umax]→{−umax,−r−1rumax,⋯,r−1rumax,umax}

which results in (2r+1)3 motion primitives.

The step is

Δu=umaxr
Actual Cost and Heuristic Cost

The objective is to find a trajectory that is optimal in time and control cost. The trajectory cost is

J(T)=∫0T∥u(t)∥2dt+ρT

For a motion primitive generated by a constant discretized input u(t)=ud and duration τ, the edge cost is

ec=(∥ud∥2+ρ)τ

If the optimal path from the start state xs to the current state xc consists of J primitives, the actual cost is

gc=∑j=1J(∥udj∥2+ρ)τ

An admissible and informative heuristic is essential to speed up the searching as in A*. A closed-form trajectory minimizing J(T) from the current state xc to the goal state xg is computed by applying Pontryagin's minimum principle

pμ∗(t)=16αμt3+12βμt2+vμct+pμc
[αμβμ]=1T3[−126T6T−2T2][pμg−pμc−vμcTvμg−vμc]
J∗(T)=∑μ∈{x,y,z}(13αμ2T3+αμβμT2+βμ2T)+ρT

where pμc, vμc, pμg, vμg are the current and goal position and velocity.

The optimal time T is obtained by substituting αμ and βμ into J∗(T) and finding the roots of

∂J∗(T)∂T=0

The root that gives a minimum cost and a feasible trajectory is selected and denoted as Th. The heuristic is

hc=J∗(Th)

Finally, fc is defined as

fc=gc+hc=gc+J∗(Th)
Analytic Expansion

Due to the discretized control input, a motion primitive can hardly end exactly at the goal state.

Therefore, an analytic expansion is used to speed up the search. When a node is popped from the open set, a closed-form trajectory from xc to xg is computed. If it is safe and dynamically feasible, the search terminates in advance.

This strategy is especially effective in sparse environments.

Optimality and Completeness

For optimality, the evaluation shows that the sacrifice of optimality is acceptable and adjustable. Moreover, if the initial path lies near the optimum, the following B-spline optimization can further improve the trajectory and find that optimum.

For completeness, the evaluation indicates that the method can find a feasible solution in most cases. The method can also be extended to support variable-duration primitives and a variable-resolution voxel grid, which can provide stronger completeness guarantees.

PMP Derivation

Pontryagin's Minimum Principle (PMP) gives necessary conditions that an optimal control input and its corresponding state trajectory must satisfy.

In other words, PMP does not directly give the final optimal trajectory by itself. Instead, it defines the mathematical conditions that any optimal solution must obey.

For the double-integrator model, the system dynamics along each axis are

p˙μ(t)=vμ(t)v˙μ(t)=uμ(t)

PMP introduces an auxiliary variable called the costate variables λp(t) and λv(t), and introduces an auxiliary function Hamiltonian.

The Hamiltonian combines the running cost and the system dynamics into one expression. The running cost describes what we want to minimize at each time, while the system dynamics describe how the state evolves under the control input.

Here, the dynamics are

[vμ(t)uμ(t)]

the running cost is

uμ2

and the costate vector is

λ(t)=[λp(t)λv(t)]

then the Hamiltonian is

H=uμ2+[λpλv][vμuμ]=uμ2+λpvμ+λvuμ

The optimal control input should minimize the Hamiltonian, so it satisfies

∂H∂uμ=2uμ+λv=0

Therefore,

uμ∗(t)=−12λv(t)

The costate equations are

λ˙p=−∂H∂pμ=0
λ˙v=−∂H∂vμ=−λp

Since

λ˙p=0

we know that λp is a constant. Let

λp=c1

Then

λ˙v=−c1

so λv(t) must be a linear function of time

λv(t)=−c1t+c2

Substituting this into the optimal control expression gives

uμ∗(t)=−12(−c1t+c2)=c12t−c22

By defining

αμ=c12,βμ=−c22

the optimal control input along each axis has a linear form for the double-integrator system

uμ∗(t)=αμt+βμ.

Since

uμ∗(t)=p¨μ∗(t)

integrating the control input twice gives the optimal position trajectory

pμ∗(t)=16αμt3+12βμt2+vμct+pμc

Here, pμc and vμc are the current position and velocity along axis μ.

The coefficients αμ and βμ are determined by enforcing the goal position and velocity constraints at time T

pμ∗(T)=pμgp˙μ∗(T)=vμg

Taking the derivative of pμ∗(t) gives

p˙μ∗(t)=12αμt2+βμt+vμc

Substituting t=T into the position and velocity equations gives

16αμT3+12βμT2=pμg−pμc−vμcT
12αμT2+βμT=vμg−vμc

Therefore, αμ and βμ can be solved from the following linear system

[16T312T212T2T][αμβμ]=[pμg−pμc−vμcTvμg−vμc]

A closed-form trajectory minimizing J(T) from the current state xc to the goal state xg is computed by applying PMP.

[αμβμ]=1T3[−126T6T−2T2][pμg−pμc−vμcTvμg−vμc]

For 3-D motion, the control cost is

∫0T∥u(t)∥2dt=∑μ∈{x,y,z}∫0T(αμt+βμ)2dt
∫0T(αμ2t2+2αμβμt+βμ2)dt=13αμ2T3+αμβμT2+βμ2T

The corresponding cost is

J∗(T)=∑μ∈{x,y,z}(13αμ2T3+αμβμT2+βμ2T)+ρT

B-spline Trajectory Optimization

Uniform B-spline

A B-spline is a piecewise polynomial uniquely determined by its degree pb, a set of N+1 control points

{Q0,Q1,⋯,QN},Qi∈R3,

and a knot vector

[t0,t1,⋯,tM],M=N+pb+1

The trajectory is parameterized by time

t∈[tpb,tM−pb]

For a uniform B-spline, every knot span has the same value

Δtm=tm+1−tm=Δt

For t∈[tm,tm+1)⊂[tpb,tM−pb], normalize time by

s(t)=t−tmΔt

The position is evaluated by the matrix representation

p(s(t))=s(t)TMpb+1qm
s(t)=[1s(t)s2(t)⋯spb(t)]T
qm=[Qm−pbQm−pb+1Qm−pb+2⋯Qm]T

Here Mpb+1 is a constant matrix determined by pb. The derivative of a B-spline is also a B-spline, so derivatives can be evaluated in the same form.

Convex Hull Property

The convex hull property is used to ensure both dynamic feasibility and safety. For dynamic feasibility, it is sufficient to constrain all velocity and acceleration control points

Vi∈[−vmax,vmax]3Ai∈[−amax,amax]3

For the uniform B-spline, the derivative control points are computed as

Vi=1Δt(Qi+1−Qi)Ai=1Δt(Vi+1−Vi)

For safety, every convex hull of the B-spline should be collision-free. Let dh be the distance between an occupied voxel and any point Qh in the convex hull. Let dc be the distance between the occupied voxel and any one control point. If r12,r23,r34 denote distances between adjacent control points in a cubic B-spline convex hull, then the triangle inequality gives

dh>dc−rhrh≤r12+r23+r34

Therefore,

dh>dc−(r12+r23+r34)

If the following condition is enforced,

dc>0rj,j+1<dc3,j∈{1,2,3}

then the convex hull is guaranteed to be collision-free.

Problem Formulation

For a pb degree B-spline trajectory defined by control points {Q0,Q1,⋯,QN}, the optimization variables are the interior N+1−2pb control points

{Qpb,Qpb+1,⋯,QN−pb}

The first and last pb control points are fixed because they determine the boundary state. The total cost is

ftotal=λ1fs+λ2fc+λ3(fv+fa)

where fs is the smoothness cost, fc is the collision cost, and fv, fa are soft limits on velocity and acceleration.

The smoothness cost captures the geometric information of the trajectory and does not depend on time allocation. It is formulated as an elastic band cost

fs=∑i=pb−1N−pb+1‖(Qi+1−Qi)⏟Fi+1,i+(Qi−1−Qi)⏟Fi−1,i‖2

This views the trajectory as an elastic band, where Fi+1,i and Fi−1,i are joint forces of two springs connected to Qi. If all terms are zero, the control points are uniformly distributed in a straight line.

The collision cost is formulated as the repulsive force of obstacles acting on each control point

fc=∑i=pbN−pbFc(d(Qi))

where d(Qi) is the distance between Qi and the closest obstacle. The differentiable potential cost function is

Fc(d(Qi))={(d(Qi)−dthr)2d(Qi)≤dthr0d(Qi)>dthr

Velocity and acceleration are penalized when they exceed the maximum allowable values. For one-dimensional velocity vμ, where μ∈{x,y,z},

Fv(vμ)={(vμ2−vmax2)2vμ2>vmax20vμ2≤vmax2

The acceleration penalty Fa has the same form. Using the convex hull property, we defines

fv=∑μ∈{x,y,z}∑i=pb−1N−pbFv(Viμ)
fa=∑μ∈{x,y,z}∑i=pb−2N−pbFa(Aiμ)

Time Adjustment

Non-uniform B-spline

A non-uniform B-spline is a more general B-spline where each knot span

Δtm=tm+1−tm

is independent to others. The first- and second-order derivative control points of a non-uniform B-spline are

Vi′=pb(Qi+1−Qi)ti+pb+1−ti+1
Ai′=(pb−1)(Vi+1′−Vi′)ti+pb+1−ti+2

By the convex hull property, enforcing all first and second order derivative control points within the feasible domain is sufficient to enforce dynamic feasibility of the whole trajectory.

Velocity Knot Span Adjustment

Let

Vi′=[Vi,x′Vi,y′Vi,z′]T

be an infeasible velocity control point. Let Vi,μ′ be the largest infeasible component and

|Vi,μ′|=vm

From the derivative formula, Vi,μ′ is influenced by the duration ti+pb+1−ti+1. If this duration is changed to

t^i+pb+1−t^i+1=μv(ti+pb+1−ti+1)

then

V^i,μ=pbt^i+pb+1−t^i+1(Qi+1,μ−Qi,μ)=1μvVi,μ′

Setting

μv=vmvmax

makes the velocity component feasible because

|V^i,μ|=vmaxvm|Vi,μ′|=vmax∈[−vmax,vmax]
Acceleration Knot Span Adjustment

For acceleration, Ai,μ′ is influenced by the knot spans associated with ti+pb+2−ti+1 because it is coupled with Vi,μ′ and Vi+1,μ′. Change

Δtm=tm+1−tm

to

Δt^m=μaΔtm,m∈{i+1,i+2,⋯,i+pb+1}

Then

A^i,μ=pb−1t^i+pb+1−t^i+2(V^i+1,μ−V^i,μ)=1μapb−1μati+pb+1−ti+2(1μaVi+1,μ′−1μaVi,μ′)=1μa2pb−1ti+pb+1−ti+2(Vi+1,μ′−Vi,μ′)=1μa2Ai,μ′

Let

μa=(amamax)12

then

|A^i,μ|=amaxam|Ai,μ′|=amax∈[−amax,amax]
Iterative Time Adjustment Algorithm
xxxxxxxxxx
repeat
    V, A ← findInfeasible()


    for each V_i' in V do
        v_m <- max_{mu ∈ {x,y,z}} |V'_{i,mu}|
        mu'_v <- min{α_v, v_m / v_max}
        AdjustKnotSpansVel(V_i', mu'_v)


    for each A_i' in A do
        a_m <- max_{mu ∈ {x,y,z}} |A'_{i,mu}|
        mu'_a <- min{α_a, sqrt(a_m / a_max)}
        AdjustKnotSpansAcc(A_i', mu'_a)


until V.empty() and A.empty()

The constants αv and αa are slightly larger than 1. They prevent any knot span from being extended excessively in a single iteration, because one knot span influences multiple derivative control points and one derivative control point is also affected by multiple knot spans.
